title: Ensure that usage is restricted and expiry is enforced for  Databricks personal access tokens

input_row.title: Ensure that usage is restricted and expiry is enforced for  Databricks personal access tokens
input_row.description: Databricks personal access tokens (PATs) provide API-based authentication for users and applications. By default, users can generate API tokens without expiration, leading to potential security risks if tokens are leaked, improperly stored, or not rotated regularly. To mitigate these risks, administrators should: • Restrict token creation to approved users and service principals. • Enforce expiration policies to prevent long-lived tokens. • Monitor token usage and revoke unused or compromised tokens.
input_row.rationale: Restricting usage and enforcing expiry for personal access tokens reduces exposure to long-lived tokens, minimizes the risk of API abuse if compromised, and aligns with security best practices through controlled issuance and enforced expiry.
input_row.audit: Azure Databricks administrators can monitor and revoke personal access tokens within their workspace. Detailed instructions are available in the "Monitor and Revoke Personal Access Tokens" section of the Microsoft documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/databricks/admin/access-control/tokens. To evaluate the usage of personal access tokens in your Azure Databricks account, you can utilize the provided notebook that lists all PATs not rotated or updated in the last 90 days, allowing you to identify tokens that may require revocation. This process is detailed here: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/databricks/admin/access- control/tokens. Implementing diagnostic logging provides a comprehensive reference of audit log services and events, enabling you to track activities related to personal access tokens. More information can be found in the diagnostic log reference section: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/databricks/admin/account-settings/audit-logs.
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal Disable personal access tokens: If your workspace does not require PATs, you can disable them entirely to prevent their use. 1. Navigate to your Azure Databricks workspace. 2. Click the Settings icon and select Admin Console. 3. Go to the Advanced tab. 4. Under Personal Access Tokens, toggle the setting to Disabled. Databricks CLI: databricks workspace-conf set-status --json '{"enableTokens": "false"}' Control who can create and use personal access tokens: Define which users or groups are authorized to create and utilize PATs. 1. Navigate to your Azure Databricks workspace. 2. Click the Settings icon and select Admin Console. 3. Go to the Advanced tab. 4. Click on Personal Access Tokens and then Permissions. 5. Assign the appropriate permissions (e.g. No Permissions, Can Use, Can Manage) to users or groups. Set maximum lifetime for new personal access tokens: Limit the validity period of new tokens to reduce potential misuse. Databricks CLI: databricks workspace-conf set-status --json '{"maxTokenLifetimeDays": "90"}' Monitor and revoke personal access tokens: Periodically review active tokens and revoke any that are unnecessary or potentially compromised. Databricks CLI: databricks token list databricks token delete --token-id Transition to OAuth for enhanced security: Utilize OAuth tokens for authentication, offering improved security features over PATs.
input_row.impact: If revoked improperly, applications relying on these tokens may fail, requiring a remediation plan for token rotation. Increased administrative effort is required to track and manage API tokens effectively.
input_row.default_value: By default, personal access tokens are enabled and users can create the Personal access token and their expiry time.
input_row.justification: The control involves managing Databricks personal access tokens, which are not directly managed by AWS services or APIs. While AWS IAM can manage user permissions, the specific management of Databricks PATs requires interaction with Databricks-specific settings and APIs, which are outside the scope of AWS automation capabilities. Therefore, a manual approach is necessary to ensure compliance with the control requirements.
input_row.manual_summary: 1. Navigate to your Azure Databricks workspace. 2. Click the Settings icon and select Admin Console. 3. Go to the Advanced tab and review the Personal Access Tokens settings. 4. Verify that the setting for enabling tokens is appropriately configured (e.g., disabled if not needed). 5. Review the list of users and service principals with permissions to create or manage PATs. 6. Ensure that expiration policies are enforced for all tokens. 7. Regularly audit the usage of PATs by checking for tokens that have not been rotated or updated in the last 90 days. 8. Document any findings and ensure that all activities related to token management are logged and stored securely for audit purposes.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Navigate to your Azure Databricks workspace.**  
   - Open the Azure Portal and select your Databricks workspace.

2. **Access Admin Console settings.**  
   - Click the Settings icon (gear icon) in the left sidebar, then select **Admin Console**.

3. **Review Personal Access Tokens settings.**  
   - Go to the **Advanced** tab in the Admin Console.
   - Check if the **Personal Access Tokens** setting is disabled (if PATs are not needed).

4. **Verify user permissions for PAT creation.**  
   - Under the **Personal Access Tokens** section, select **Permissions**.
   - Review and verify which users or service principals have permissions to create/use PATs. Ensure only approved users are listed.

5. **Check expiration policies for PATs.**  
   - Confirm if a maximum lifetime for new personal access tokens is set (e.g., 90 days or less). If there isn’t one, record this as a compliance gap.

6. **Audit the usage of PATs.**  
   - Review the list of personal access tokens within the workspace for any that have not been rotated or updated in the last 90 days.
   - Identify and document any tokens that may require revocation.

7. **Document findings.**  
   - Record your observations, including any tokens that need attention and ensure that this documentation is stored securely for audit purposes.

8. **Confirm logging and monitoring configurations.**  
   - Finally, ensure that diagnostic logging is enabled for token management to track activities related to personal access tokens.

By following these steps, you will be able to manually verify compliance with the control regarding Azure Databricks personal access tokens.
